title: Path Planning and Obstacle Avoidance Optimization for a Spraying Robot Based on an Improved Artificial Potential Field Algorithm
authors: Tengfei Zheng, Long Liu, Yahao Kuang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Mechanical Engineering, Intelligent Manufacturing, and Automation Technology (MEMAT)
abstract: To mitigate the risk of collisions during the automated spraying of complex components such as port equipment, this study proposes a path planning and obstacle avoidance optimization algorithm that integrates the Artificial Potential Field (APF) method with the Rapidly-exploring Random Tree (RRT) algorithm. The proposed method addresses key limitations of conventional APF approaches in complex environments, including entrapment in local minima, inaccessibility of target positions, and path oscillations. To ensure path feasibility, the algorithm incorporates a failure detection mechanism that triggers localized RRT-based path replanning when APF fails, thereby enhancing target reachability. Additionally, a path smoothing process is applied to improve the overall trajectory quality. Simulation experiments conducted on a small-scale balance beam model with embedded obstacles demonstrate that the proposed hybrid algorithm can effectively avoid collisions and generate smooth, feasible spraying trajectories, thereby overcoming the drawbacks of traditional approaches. This work offers a robust path planning strategy and valuable technical reference for enhancing the intelligence and safety of robotic spraying operations.

title: Research on Linear-Switch Hybrid Power Hardware-in-the-Loop Simulation Technology
authors: Huaiguang Gu, Zhiwei Mi, Longze Kou, Feng Ji
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Energy, Power and Electrical Engineering (EPEE)
abstract: The new power system topology, control, fault evolution, and other processes characterized by “double high” exhibit broadband, fast, nonlinear, and complex phenomena, posing severe challenges to simulation, testing, and verification technologies. Power Hardware in the Loop (PHIL) combines the convenience of real-time digital simulation with the authenticity of physical dynamic testing, bringing great flexibility to the research and testing of new power electronic equipment. In order to solve the challenges of wide voltage, high power, high bandwidth, high precision, high voltage swing rate, low delay and high requirements in PHIL simulation technology, this paper proposes a linear switch hybrid four quadrant power hardware in the loop simulation technology, and verifies it in electromagnetic transient simulation software PSCAD/EMTDC. The verification results show that the technology has certain feasibility.

title: Improved Pied Kingfisher Optimization for Wireless Power Transfer Parameter Tuning
authors: Mengjiang Wu, Ying Jiang
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Energy, Power and Electrical Engineering (EPEE)
abstract: The Pied Kingfisher optimization algorithm is a novel single-objective meta-heuristic algorithm, which has excellent global search ability and fast convergence. Single-objective optimization algorithms are difficult to handle the trade-offs among multiple objectives, which limits their application in complex real-world decision-making scenarios. LCC/S-WPT systems are typically dynamic, with varying load and coupling conditions. The compensation topological parameters designed by the traditional resonant method can only ensure a constant voltage under fixed coupling conditions. Dynamic load and coupling changes can lead to significant voltage fluctuations and efficiency drops. Therefore, this paper proposes the MOPKO algorithm to optimize the parameters of the WPT system. The system parameters designed by the MOPKO algorithm have improved the output voltage stability by approximately 50% compared to those designed by the traditional resonant method under dynamically changing coupling coefficients and load conditions.

title: Model Context Protocol-based Internet of Experts For Wireless Environment-aware LLM Agents
authors: Zongxi Liu, Hongyang Du
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit strong general-purpose reasoning abilities but lack access to wire-less environment information due to the absence of native sensory input and domain-specific priors. Previous attempts to apply LLMs in wireless systems either depend on retraining with network-specific data, which compromises language generalization, or rely on manually scripted interfaces, which hinder scalability. To overcome these limitations, we propose a Model Context Protocol (MCP)-based Internet of Experts (IoX) framework that equips LLMs with wireless environment-aware reasoning capabilities. The framework incorporates a set of lightweight expert models, each trained to solve a specific deterministic task in wireless communications, such as detecting a specific wireless attribute, e.g., line-of-sight propagation, Doppler effects, or fading conditions. Through MCP, the LLM can selectively query and interpret expert outputs at inference time, without modifying its own parameters. This architecture enables modular, extensible, and interpretable reasoning over wireless contexts. Evaluated across multiple mainstream LLMs, the proposed wireless environment-aware LLM agents achieve 40%-50% improvements in classification tasks over LLM-only baselines. More broadly, the MCP-based design offers a viable paradigm for future LLMs to inherit structured wireless network management capabilities.

title: TinyFLChain: Blockchain-Secured Decentralized Federated Learning for TinyML Systems
authors: Douaa El Achhab, Anouar Zouhri, Meryeme Ayache, Nidal Nasser
abstract: The rise of embedded AI has introduced critical challenges, including limited RAM, storage, and energy consumption. Emerging trends, such as TinyML, offer promising solutions to these constraints. In this work, we introduce TinyFLChain, a decentralized framework that combines TinyML, Federated Learning, and blockchain to enable privacy-preserving and secure collaborative learning on low-power IoT devices. By leveraging FL, computations are decentralized and data remains on local nodes, while blockchain ensures the integrity and traceability of model updates through smart contracts. Our framework utilizes blockchain smart contracts to secure the exchange of models updates, and implement a performance-based incentive mechanism. Clients who provide high-quality updates are rewarded at each round, while less active are penalized, encouraging sustained engagement. Using Raspberry Pi devices, we evaluated TinyFLChain on three benchmark datasets (MNIST, CIFAR-10, Digits). In centralised system, the accuracy reaches 100% on MNIST, 87% on CIFAR-10, and 97% on Digits. In decentralised system without blockchain, the performance is 99.25% on MNIST, 38.9% on CIFAR-10, and 10.72% on Digits. With TinyFLChain, accuracy significantly improves on CIFAR-10, reaching 62.09% for the best client, while remaining stable on MNIST. The results show that our system maintains high accuracy while improving security, transparency, and robustness against attacks.

title: Communication-Efficient Multi-Agent Collaborative Perception via Spatio-Temporal Heterogeneity
authors: Yilei Wang, Peng Sun, Jingyu Zhang, Lang Qian, Azzedine Boukerche, Liang Song
abstract: Multi-agent collaborative perception enables a single agent to perceive the comprehensive scene by exchanging sensory information using vehicle-to-everything communication. However, the communication resources of real-world communication systems are often limited. It fails to satisfy the real-time transmission of extensive data, which restricts the deployment of collaborative perception. To address the issue, we propose ComSH, a Communication-efficient multi-agent collaborative perception method based on Spatio-temporal Heterogeneity to achieve a trade-off between performance and bandwidth. Specifically, we consider the spatio-temporal heterogeneity to perform feature filtering, thus reducing the redundancy of transmitted features. First, it introduces valuable temporal semantics to enhance the current representation of each agent. Secondly, we consider the spatial confidence of each agent at each location and the relative position to obtain high confidence and complementary features. Eventually, a foreground object adaptive activation module is designed to enrich the visual representation of fused features. Therefore, ComSH enables different agents to transmit their critical and complementary perception information, thus reducing bandwidth consumption. We conduct experiments on collaborative detection tasks on the two datasets. Experimental results demonstrate the ComSH’s superiority and effectiveness.

title: Observer-Based Robust H ∞ Fault-Tolerant Control for T-S Fuzzy Fractional-Order Systems With Multiple Faults and Disturbances
authors: Zhijie Han, Huaguang Zhang, Yunfei Mu, Weihua Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: The robust H∞ fault-tolerant control problem for a class of nonlinear fractional-order systems described by T-S fuzzy models is investigated in this paper. A transformed system is firstly constructed according to the original system. Then, a fractional-order intermediate observer is proposed. It can obtain the state and actuator fault of the transformed system, which indirectly achieves the simultaneous estimation of states, faults and disturbances of the original system. Unlike the strategy of suppressing or decoupling the disturbances in traditional fault estimation methods of nonlinear fractional-order systems, this paper focuses on estimating the disturbances directly. This way not only avoids handling the disturbances or the dynamics of faults via the H∞ method, but also does not have strict equation constraints required in the decoupling method. Additionally, an active fault-tolerant controller is designed that dynamically adjusts its actions based on the information obtained by the observer, thereby ensuring system stability under multiple faults and disturbances. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed method is validated on two typical application circuits.

title: An E-Band Blocker-Tolerant Receiver in 0.18-μm SiGe BiCMOS
authors: Erez Zolkov, Emanuel Cohen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems II: Express Briefs
abstract: This letter presents a highly linear, E-band receiver (RX) intended for digital beam-forming applications. It achieves high out-of-band (OOB) blocker tolerance without sacrificing performance or power, by utilizing a highly linear, single-stage low-noise transconductance amplifier (LNTA), followed by an N-path mixer with an embedded band-pass filtering and tunable center frequency, and a baseband (BB) transimpedance amplifier for linearity enhancement. The LNTA and N-path mixer design equations are derived, and their trade-offs are discussed. A chip prototype was manufactured in Tower 0.18-μm SiGe BiCMOS process. In our implementation, 6.5-12.5 dB noise figure is achieved, with 2 GHz RF channel bandwidth, 26-35 dB RX gain, -18 dBm in-band IIP3 and -6 dBm B1dB at a 2 GHz offset, while occupying an active area of 0.45 mm2 and drawing total power of 138 mW, in the frequency range of 72-90 GHz.

title: A Numerical Synthesis Method of Isolation Characterized Three-Port Filtering Power Divider Network and Its Application
authors: Gang Zhang, Minghan Shu, Peiyuan Du, Xinni Hu, Kam Weng Tam, Zhuowei Zhang
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: In this article, a numerical synthesis method is proposed to extract the coupling matrix of an isolation characterized three-port filtering power divider (TP-FPD) network, for the first time. This method effectively facilitates the design of TP-FPD with consideration of port-to-port isolation. In the synthesis process, a newly defined coupling matrix is developed, which clearly represents the isolation characteristics of the network from the corresponding FPD topology. The coupling matrix is synthesized by optimizing an isolation cost function tailored to the TP-FPD topology, thereby circumventing the complex process of similar transformations typically required for the coupling matrix. Finally, the proposed synthesis method is well verified, and two practical models are designed to verify the validity of the isolation characterized coupling matrix derived from the proposed method.

title: SR-VIO: Leveraging Structural Regularity for Robust Initialization and Accurate Visual-Inertial Odometry_supp1-3669015.pdf
authors: huilin yin
abstract: Visual-inertial odometry (VIO) is pivotal for ego-motion estimation in autonomous mobile systems. However, Structure from Motion (SfM)-based initialization exhibits robustness degradation under low-parallax conditions, while structurally challenging scenes (e.g., textureless regions) may lead to local trajectory drift. A robust VIO is proposed to estimate the motion state of RGB-D cameras, which fully leverages structural regularity throughout the entire pipeline. First, to achieve more reliable visual-inertial initialization, especially under extreme motion, the geometric regularity of coplanar normals between different observation planes in two frames is used to stabilize the estimation of gyroscope bias. Second, for better detection of structural constraints in the environment, a loosely coupled gravitational vector-optimized method aligned with the globally unique axis in the Atlanta World is proposed. An initial state solver based on linear global translation constraints is integrated into the system to achieve rapid and accurate estimation of the gravity axis. Particularly, a frame-to-frame discriminative method using line feature endpoint reprojection identifies outlier lines in structural clusters and enhances efficiency in correlating line features with Atlanta axes. In addition, a depth covariance intersection filter in VIO initialization and optimization ensures consistent, optimal fusion results despite sensor noise and complex lighting. Experiments demonstrate that the proposed method has faster and more accurate initialization state estimation results and better positioning accuracy in various complex structural environments compared to state-of-the-art simultaneous localization and mapping systems.

title: IntactEdge: Secure Multi-Replica Data Integrity Verification in Mobile Edge Computing
authors: Lingshuang Liu, Liang Xue, Xiangman Li, Xuemin Sherman Shen
abstract: In this paper, we propose a new secure data integrity verification scheme for mobile edge computing (MEC), named IntactEdge, which provides an efficient method for end users in MEC to verify that the edge servers correctly maintain their stored data. Particularly, IntactEdge is designed using lightweight cryptographic mechanisms, such as message authentication codes and hash functions, to ensure efficiency in generating and verifying data integrity proofs. Batch verification is also supported to further enhance computational efficiency for end users during integrity checks. In addition, IntactEdge offers the desirable feature of multi-replica secure storage, allowing user data to be distributed across multiple edge servers to enhance data availability. To prevent edge nodes from exploiting data replicas stored on other nodes to falsely satisfy integrity verification requests, IntactEdge generates distinct data replicas for different edge nodes, while still enabling each replica to support proof generation using the same authentication tag. Finally, we demonstrate that IntactEdge is secure against potential data integrity attacks on edge servers and highly efficient in both tag generation and integrity verification.

title: Method for detecting discontinuities in aluminum castings based on a Self-Excited Acoustic System and deep learning techniques
authors: Jan Przepióra, Piotr Ksi˛a˙zek
abstract: This work deals with detection of material discontinuities and inclusions in aluminum castings via a Self-Excited Acoustic System through application of artificial intelligence methods. Various feature extraction methods were compared, including mel-frequency cepstral coefficients (MFCC), short-time Fourier transform (STFT), power spectral density (PSD), and both the discrete and continuous wavelet transforms. The study evaluates the efficacy of each method in capturing essential information for inclusion detection within acoustic signals. In addition, principal component analysis (PCA) was employed to reduce dimensionality. The findings contribute to the advancement of quality control techniques for the production of such systems through robust and efficient detection mechanisms. Experimental results demonstrated that CNNs combined with STFT as well as MFCCs achieved the highest accuracy, exceeding 94% classification accuracy. Networks trained on one-dimensional data showed slightly lower but still robust performance. The feature importance analysis revealed that frequencies between 2 kHz and 12 kHz carried the most significant information for defect detection. These findings confirm that the integration of self-excited acoustic measurements with both deep learning and classical machine learning approaches enables reliable, non destructive identification of discontinuities in aluminum castings.

title: MCIB: Multi-modal Complementary Information Bottleneck for Hyperspectral and LiDAR Classification
authors: Xiao Pan, Hao Zhu, Bo Yang, Changzhe Jiao, Jie Feng, Jinjian Wu
abstract: The effective fusion of multi-modal remote sensing images, particularly hyperspectral imagery (HSI) and light detection and ranging (LiDAR) data, is pivotal for accurate land use and land cover (LULC) classification. However, this process is hindered by two inherent challenges: pervasive data redundancy and the underutilization of cross-modal complementarity, largely due to the lack of a unifying theoretical framework. To address these limitations, we propose the multi-modal complementary information bottleneck (MCIB) framework, which extends the IB principle to learn compact, sufficient, and complementary representations for multi-modal scenes. From a theoretical perspective, we formalize the MCIB objective and introduce structured priors to derive tractable information-theoretic bounds, providing a principled and computationally feasible approach to reduce redundancy and enhance complementarity simultaneously. Building on the obtained theoretical insights, we design an end-to-end variational optimization strategy with a novel supervised conditional InfoNCE (SCInfoNCE). Efficiently reusing existing model components, this new supervised contrastive method optimizes the conditional mutual information terms crucial for synergy. Extensive experiments on benchmark HSI-LiDAR datasets demonstrate superior classification performance of MCIB. This work not only fills a theoretical gap in multi-modal representation learning, but offers a robust and principled solution for LULC classification using complex heterogeneous remote sensing images.

title: A Joint UAV Deployment and Beamforming Design for ISAC-Enabled Multi-UAV Network
authors: Xiaoye Jing, Fan Liu, Christos Masouros, Xianhua Yu
abstract: This paper exploits the integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) technology in unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) networks, where multiple UAVs collaboratively form a virtual antenna array (VAA) within a pre-determined area, to effectively operate as a multi-antenna system for communication and sensing (C&S) services. Since the VAA is an extremely-large antenna array, the near-field characteristics must be considered in C&S channels. By optimizing UAV positions, we construct an enhanced VAA configuration and subsequently design the corresponding beamforming, thereby improving sensing performance while guaranteeing communication requirements. A penalty-based iterative algorithm is exploited to address the resulting optimization problem. Simulation results demonstrate that the optimized VAA with beamforming significantly enhances target localization accuracy compared to conventional schemes, validating the effectiveness of the ISAC implementation in UAV networks.

title: Disturbance Observer-Based Control in the Synchronous Reference Frame for Grid-Connected Inverters in the Presence of Grid-Voltage Harmonics
authors: Mahdi Debouza, Rachid Errouissi, Hussain Shareef
abstract: This paper proposes the design and testing of disturbance observer-based current control for L-type grid-connected voltage source inverter (VSI) considering grid voltage distortion. In particular, the proposed current controller is designed to ensure the injection of high-quality currents into the grid despite the presence of grid voltage harmonics. In this work, the current controller is derived in the synchronously rotating (dq) frame to allow for one-to-two harmonics compensation. In such a frame, a disturbance observer is designed to estimate the effect of selected voltage harmonics, which is then compensated for by a feedback control law. Nevertheless, synthesizing a stable disturbance observer to cope with a large number of harmonics is still challenging. The main challenge in observer design lies in selecting suitable observer gains, whose dimensionality increases as the number of voltage harmonics increases. This challenge in selecting the observer gains is addressed in this work by using the concept of D-stability together with the linear matrix inequality (LMI) approach. This approach reformulates the stability problem of the observer into solving specific LMIs offline such that all observer eigenvalues are confined within a stable region D, which can be selected according to desired transient performance. An additional insight is that the resulting current controller structurally reduces to a proportional-integral (PI) controller augmented with a multi-resonant (MR) compensator, thus facilitating practical implementation. Laboratory-scale experimental results confirm the efficacy of the proposed controller in achieving good dynamic performance with accurate mitigation of harmonic voltage disturbances.

title: Deep Source Separation with Prior
authors: Rodrigo Da Silva Cassimiro, Kenji Nose-Filho, Ricardo Suyama
abstract: Blind Source Separation (BSS) methods are used in digital signal processing to recover latent sources from a set of observed mixed signals, with little or no prior knowledge of the sources or the mixing process. Sparse Component Analysis (SCA) methods are particularly effective for addressing underdetermined cases, where the number of sources exceeds the number of observed mixtures, assuming that the mixtures are instantaneous linear combinations of the sources. These methods generally follow a two-stage approach: first, estimate the mixing matrix; second, reconstruct the original sources. The latter stage is particularly challenging, especially in the presence of noise, because it can introduce undesirable distortions and artifacts in the recovered signals. In this work, we investigate the use of a Deep Image Prior (DIP) convolutional neural network, adapted to handle one-dimensional signals, for the reconstruction stage of latent sources in the underdetermined case with instantaneous mixtures. Experimental results show that the proposed approach outperforms traditional SCA methods based on ℓ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sub> norm minimization, particularly in scenarios involving non-overlapping sources and mixtures heavily contaminated with additive noise.

title: AI-driven State Feedback Power Control Design of Boost Converters for Renewable Energy Applications Using a HIL Implementation
authors: Rodrigo Trentini, Eduardo Rochinski Arbigaus, Yuri Roberto Ferreira, Davi Bernardo Silva, Rodrigo José Piontkewicz, Diego Santos Greff
abstract: The integration of renewable energy sources, such as photovoltaic (PV) and wind power plants, into electrical grids poses significant challenges in maintaining stability due to the lack of natural inertia in these systems. This paper addresses the critical need for precise power injection control from renewable sources to mitigate frequency instability, exemplified by events like the recent blackout in Brazil. Building on AI-driven methodologies, the study proposes a novel strategy for designing state space feedback controllers for boost converters in PV systems. Specifically, the research leverages Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) to optimize the training of Machine Learning (ML) models, with a focus on Linear Regression (LR), to ensure robust and adaptive controller performance. The proposed approach is validated using a hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) platform that combines a Typhoon HIL 404 real-time simulator and an NI myRIO embedded controller, analysing its efficiency, input and output current and voltage levels, controller signal behaviour, dynamic response, and stabilization time. Results demonstrate the potential of integrating PSO-optimized ML models to enhance power converter performance, offering a practical framework for improving power injection control in renewable energy systems. This work underscores the transformative role of AI in addressing critical challenges in grid stability and renewable energy integration.

title: Next-Gen Smart Win-Curtain: A Real-Time IoT &#x0026; Fuzzy Based Implementation for Future Homes_supp1-3674134.pdf
authors: Ankur Singhai
abstract: Residential and commercial buildings remain highly dependent on manually operated or fixed window curtains, leading to inefficient daylight utilization, increased artificial lighting consumption, and compromised indoor comfort. In practical smart home deployments, most automated curtain solutions rely on limited sensing or user-driven control, which restricts their adaptability in changing indoor conditions. To address these limitations, the proposed system adaptively regulates curtain position and indoor lighting using fuzzy logic and ZigBee based wireless communication. The system continuously monitors indoor conditions through multiple environmental sensors measuring ambient light, occupancy, temperature, and air quality. A Mamdani-type fuzzy inference engine implemented on embedded microcontrollers processes the sensor data and generates smooth control actions for curtain and lighting adjustment. Experimental evaluation using a hardware prototype demonstrates fast system response and stable operation under varying daylight conditions. The results show that the proposed approach can reduce artificial lighting energy consumption by up to 40% during daytime operation while maintaining occupant comfort and indoor environmental quality. The developed smart win-curtain system offers a practical and scalable solution for intelligent and sustainable smart home automation.

title: Filling in the Gap of Eliminating CMV Spikes Under Low Modulation Indices in the SVM Three-Phase Inverters
authors: Jin Huang, Kaicheng Li
abstract: The high common-mode voltage (CMV) in two-level three-phase inverters will bring many negative effects. Many strategies are studied to reduce the CMV and eliminate the CMV spikes. However, due to the switching dead-time, there is a gap in eliminating CMV spikes when the inverter works under low modulation indices (the modulation index Ma < 0.4) with the existing space-vector modulation (SVM) strategies for reducing CMV (RCMV). This paper proposes a new strategy named all non-zero vectors SVM (ANZVSVM) to fill in the gap. In this strategy, as three pairs of reverse non-zero vectors are used to equivalent the zero vectors, the dwell times of the original middle-order vectors can be effectively prolonged under low modulation indices. Thus, the new strategy has the ability to eliminate the CMV spikes when the Ma is low enough to approach zero. This characteristic is not only proven in theory, but also verified by the simulations and experiments in a three-phase inverter. Compared to other RCMV strategies in the inverter under the low modulation indices, the ANZVSVM strategy has significantly lower THD and lower common-mode current peaks in both time-domain and frequency-domain.

title: Mitigating NDR for Improved RF Performance in $\mathrm{MoS_{2}}$ Ferroelectric Tunnel FET
authors: Jagritee Talukdar, Bhaskaran Muralidharan
abstract: The work realized a steep-slope <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathrm{MoS_{2}}$</tex-math></inline-formula> based negative capacitance-tunnel field effect transistor (NC-TMDTFET) through the incorporation of the ferroelectric layer into the gate stack. The proposed device sustained a subthreshold slope of 0.023V/decade and 0.018V/decade in forward and reverse sweep respectively and an <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathrm{ON/OFF}$</tex-math></inline-formula> current ratio of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$10^{12}$</tex-math></inline-formula>. The work investigates the effect of the ferroelectric layer on input and output characteristics. In the subsequent observation, it was noted that as the drain voltage increases, there is a decrease in current, indicating the presence of negative differential resistance (NDR) within the proposed device. However, mitigation of such effect in TMD based NC-TFET has not yet been introduced. Hence, to address the NDR issue hetero-dielectric BOX (H-BOX) is introduced which directly influences the gate-to-drain capacitance of the device. We report a 56.71% reduction in <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$C_{GD}$</tex-math></inline-formula>, a 15.83% improvement in intrinsic delay, and a corresponding 48.95% improvement in cutoff frequency due to the introduction of H-BOX resulting enhancement in device speed. These improvements demonstrate that H-BOX integration effectively enhances key parameters making it a viable solution for analog applications that require high performance and low power consumption.

title: Sample-Efficient Learning of a Robotic Tube Insertion Policy
authors: Asheer Bachoo, Hamidreza Azimian, Kento Okumura, Marc Morcos
abstract: Reinforcement learning for manipulation with real robots is complicated when faced with object variations and contact dynamics. Further, achieving support across a category of objects for manipulation is in itself a challenging task. A recent work that addresses training inefficiencies on a real robot is the Contrastive Pre-training and Data Augmentation for Efficient Robotic Learning (CoDER) framework. It uses a combination of contrastive-pretraining, data augmentation, expert demonstrations and reinforcement learning to achieve sample-efficiency. To improve sample-efficiency further, we used force/torque inputs, pre-trained image encoders, layer normalization, and we warmed up our actor-critic networks before training. In this work, we propose these improvements to the original CoDER for stable and efficient training of a real robot for fine manipulation - we insert medical tubes into a rack. Our policy is trained on a single tube and it is able to support different tubes in the test set. The experimental results show an average insertion success rate of 98.1% across several different types of medical tubes. We achieve this robustness with a policy trained in only 800 episodes over approximately 6 hours using sparse rewards.

title: A Data Center Wireless-Optical Interconnect Architecture Based on Cascaded Meta-Surfaces
authors: Weijie Qiu, Weigang Hou, Xiaoxue Gong, Lei Guo
abstract: The existing wireless-optical switching units comprise spatial light modulators (SLM) and micro-electromechanical systems (MEMS), which steer the beam from a server port in a certain rack to another rack, establishing an inter-rack communication link. Nevertheless, this uni-cast communication approach results in a limited switching capacity. Fortunately, our passive reflective meta-surfaces (RMSs) demonstrate excellent beam-splitting capabilities, that is, after an incident light is reflected by our RMS, a pair of reflected beams can be simultaneously formed. In this paper, we further design a transmissive meta-surface (TMS), and after one incident light transmits through the TMS, N beams can be formed. Each of the N beams is subsequently reflected by the corresponding RMS, and ultimately, we can obtain 2×N beams. As a result, it significantly enhances the switching capacity of data centers (DCs). Simulation results validate the one-to-four (N=4) beam-splitting capacity of our TMS, with the insertion loss being as low as 0.5dB, efficiently being cascaded with RMS.

title: Development of Worm gear-based Series Elastic Actuator for Torque-efficient Vibration Isolation
authors: Kyeongsik Shin, Sehoon Oh
publish: 2026 IEEE 19th International Conference on Advanced Motion Control (AMC)
abstract: Vibration isolation is essential for comfort and safety in autonomous and electric vehicles, yet conventional active systems are often constrained by high energy consumption. To address this, this paper proposes a Worm gear-based Series Elastic Actuator (WSEA) designed for torque-efficient vibration isolation. The proposed actuator integrates a worm gear mechanism with a series elastic element. By leveraging the inherent self-locking property of the worm gear, the WSEA realizes a powerless brake mechanism that prevents back-drivability and supports external loads without continuous motor torque. Furthermore, a switching control algorithm is developed to energy efficiency by switching between active force control for vibration suppression and the self-locking brake mode during low-disturbance periods. Simulation results based on ISO Class D road profiles demonstrate that the proposed WSEA system effectively isolates vertical vibrations while significantly reducing torque consumption compared to conventional active control methods. This study suggests a promising approach for energy-efficient suspension systems in future mobility platforms.

title: Evaluating the Impact of Remote Work Benefits on Employee Performance and Retention in IT Companies
authors: Anitha Kumari D, Tanushri Purohit, Chitkala Venkareddy, Pratibha Giri, R Gayathri, M. Dinesh Babu
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Advances in Computation, Communication and Information Technology (ICAICCIT)
abstract: The COVID-19 pandemic and other global events that changed the way people work have made remote work more common, especially in IT companies. More and more businesses are using remote employee benefits (REB) to keep good workers, boost productivity, and make people happier at work. Wellness programs, technology allowances, and the option to work flexible hours are all part of this group of benefits. There isn't much real-world proof of how certain REBs affect employee outcomes in IT companies, such as performance, engagement, and turnover rates, even though they are widely used. This study uses a combination of MultiCriteria Decision-Making (MCDM) and other methods to find out how a number of REB affect things. To be more specific, the Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) and Fuzzy Logic are used together. The survey had 250 workers from five different multinational information technology companies. The hybrid AHP-Fuzzy model found that the best things for employees to do their jobs better and stay with the company the longest were flexible work hours (0.35), mental health support (0.27), and remote infrastructure allowances (0.21). In comparison, traditional benefits like travel allowances and perks for physical workspaces didn't have as big of an effect. The proposed method was much better than the standard Likert scale and linear regression analysis at giving results that were both accurate and easy to understand.

title: The Price of Privacy: Quantifying the Impact on Localization Accuracy in Underwater Secure Localization
authors: Rong Fan, Azzedine Boukerche, Sidan Yang, Zhigang Jin, Yishan Su
abstract: A persistent risk in many underwater localization solutions is the leakage of position information, since anchor positions are revealed to the sensors performing position estimation. While cryptography-based secure localization mechanisms for underwater acoustic sensor networks (UASNs) can protect the privacy of anchors, varying levels of privacy preservation impact localization accuracy. Therefore, it is necessary to derive a quantitative relationship between privacy preservation levels and localization accuracy. In this paper, we first model the probability density function (PDF) of anchor decoding errors under privacy preservation constraints when anchors adopt an encrypted system to safeguard position information. The model parameters are optimized using the expectation-maximization (EM) algorithm. Next, based on the ranging PDF and anchor decoding error PDF distribution, we derive the position error bound (PEB) function that links privacy preservation levels with target localization accuracy. Simulation and field experiment results validate the effectiveness of the theoretical model.

title: Privacy Assessment of Text-to-Image Models via Enhanced Membership Inference Attack
authors: Ben Niu, Jiaqi Liu, Likun Zhang, Yiming Jiang, Yuqiao Hou, Jin Cao
abstract: Text-to-image diffusion models, such as Stable Diffusion and DALL-E, can generate attractive and high-quality images for various applications. However, their training procedures typically depend on large-scale image-text paired datasets, raising concerns about potential privacy leakage such as test-time replication of training data. To quantify the training data memorization in text-to-image models, we introduce a privacy assessment framework through the lens of an enhanced membership inference method. It involves iteratively refining text prompts through multiple optimization cycles to identify input formulations that yield maximally aligned output images. Building on these optimized results, we propose a visual-semantic fusion metric for assessing model memorization. We extensively evaluate the effectiveness of our assessment framework. Experimental results demonstrate that our evaluation framework successfully quantifies both the extent of training data retention and privacy risks, achieving overall 90% detection accuracy in validation tests. Meanwhile, our method significantly outperforms the baseline membership inference methods, which largely underestimate privacy leakage risk of text-to-image models.

title: Base Station Placement Optimization for Networked Sensing Exploiting Target Location Distribution
authors: Kaiyue Hou, Shuowen Zhang
abstract: This paper studies a networked sensing system with multiple base stations (BSs), which collaboratively sense the unknown and random three-dimensional (3D) location of a target based on the target-reflected echo signals received at the BSs. Considering a practical scenario where the target location distribution is known a priori for exploitation, we aim to design the placement of the multiple BSs to optimize the networked sensing performance. Firstly, we characterize the posterior Cramér-Rao bound (PCRB) of the mean-squared error (MSE) in sensing the target’s 3D location. Despite its complex form under networked sensing, we derive its closed-form expression in terms of the BS locations. Next, we formulate the BS placement optimization problem to minimize the sensing PCRB, which is non-convex and difficult to solve. By leveraging a series of equivalent transformations and the iterative inner approximation method, we devise an algorithm with polynomial-time complexity which is guaranteed to converge to a solution satisfying the Karush-Kuhn Tucker (KKT) conditions of the problem. Numerical results show that the proposed placement design significantly outperforms various benchmark designs.

title: Design of a 300W Phase-Shifted Full-Bridge Secondary Power Supply Module for Aerospace Satellite Applications
authors: Chenxu Zhu, Weizheng Cheng, Zheng Gao, Yang Zhao, Yuke Gong, Zhenbo Mu
publish: 2025 International Conference on Mechanical Engineering, Intelligent Manufacturing, and Automation Technology (MEMAT)
abstract: With the application and development of satellites with diverse functionalities, the complexity of satellite power supply systems has increased alongside mission demands. To meet payload requirements for onboard secondary power sources, a phase-shifted full-bridge soft-switching and synchronous rectification main circuit topology was adopted. The UC1875 microcontroller was selected as the main control chip, and magnetic component designs were specifically optimized to achieve wide-range soft switching. A prototype power module with 42 V input and 300W/12V output was developed, achieving a power density of 59.3 W/in<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup> and a peak efficiency of 92.9%. Simulation and experimental results validated the correctness and feasibility of the design.
